class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Statistics and Data Types
learning_objectives: Distinguish between descriptive and inferential statistics
Classify data as quantitative or qualitative, discrete or continuous
Identify levels of measurement: nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio
readings: How to Lie with Statistics Ch. 1-2 by Darrell Huff

lecture_notes.introduction: Statistics is the science of collecting, organizing, analyzing, and interpreting data to make informed decisions. In a world drowning in data, statistical literacy is not optional.
key_concepts: Population vs. sample
Descriptive statistics: summarize data
Inferential statistics: draw conclusions about populations from samples
Qualitative (categorical) vs. quantitative (numerical) data
Four levels of measurement and what operations each allows
lecture_notes.demonstration: Classifying real datasets: census data, survey responses, and sensor readings by type and level of measurement

review.value: Nominal (categories only), Ordinal (ranked categories), Interval (equal intervals, no true zero), Ratio (equal intervals with true zero)
Population: the entire group of interest. Sample: a subset used to make inferences about the population. Parameters describe populations; statistics describe samples.

learning_objectives: Calculate mean, median, and mode for a dataset
Compute range, variance, and standard deviation
Interpret what measures of center and spread reveal about data distribution
readings: Standard Deviation: Why It Matters (supplementary article)

lecture_notes.introduction: Descriptive statistics reduce large datasets to a few meaningful numbers. But which numbers to report depends on the data distribution, and choosing the wrong measure can be deeply misleading.
key_concepts: Mean: arithmetic average, sensitive to outliers
Median: middle value, resistant to outliers
Mode: most frequent value, useful for categorical data
Range, variance, and standard deviation
When mean vs. median tells different stories: skewed distributions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating descriptive statistics for CEO salaries vs. median household income and discussing why the median is preferred

review.value: Measures average distance of data points from the mean. Small SD means data is clustered; large SD means data is spread out.
In right-skewed data, mean > median (pulled by high outliers). Report median for skewed data, mean for symmetric data.

class_schedule.topic: Data Visualization: Charts, Graphs, and Distributions
learning_objectives: Create and interpret histograms, box plots, and scatter plots
Identify the shape of a distribution: symmetric, skewed, bimodal
Detect misleading visualizations and design honest ones
readings: Calling Bullshit Ch. 7 by Bergstrom & West

lecture_notes.introduction: A well-designed graph can reveal patterns invisible in raw numbers. A poorly designed one can deceive. This session teaches both creation and critical reading of data visualizations.
key_concepts: Histograms: frequency distribution of continuous data
Box plots: five-number summary and outlier detection
Scatter plots: visualizing relationships between two variables
Distribution shapes: normal, uniform, skewed, bimodal
Misleading graph techniques and how to spot them
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a histogram, box plot, and scatter plot from the same dataset using spreadsheet software

review.value: Shows minimum, Q1, median, Q3, maximum. The box spans the interquartile range (IQR). Points beyond 1.5 × IQR from Q1 or Q3 are outliers.
Bell-shaped and symmetric. Mean = median = mode. About 68% of data within 1 SD, 95% within 2 SD, 99.7% within 3 SD.

learning_objectives: Calculate probabilities using classical, empirical, and subjective approaches
Apply the addition and multiplication rules for probability
Understand the concept of expected value

lecture_notes.introduction: Probability is the mathematical backbone of statistical inference. To understand why a sample tells us something about a population, we must understand the laws of chance.
key_concepts: Classical probability: equally likely outcomes
Empirical probability: based on observed frequency
Addition rule: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)
Multiplication rule for independent events: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B)
Expected value: long-run average outcome
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulating coin flips and dice rolls to compare theoretical and empirical probability as sample size increases
setup_requirements: Dice and coins for physical simulation (provided)

review.value: As the number of trials increases, the empirical probability approaches the theoretical probability
E(X) = Σ[x × P(x)]. Represents the long-run average. A game with negative expected value loses money over time.

learning_objectives: Use the standard normal table to find probabilities
Apply the empirical rule and normal distribution to real-world data
readings: The Normal Distribution in Nature (supplementary reading)

lecture_notes.introduction: The normal distribution is the most important probability distribution in statistics. It appears naturally in measurements of height, test scores, measurement errors, and countless other phenomena.
key_concepts: The normal curve: bell-shaped, symmetric, defined by mean and standard deviation
Z-score: z = (x - μ) / σ, measures how many standard deviations from the mean
Standard normal table: finding area under the curve
Empirical rule: 68-95-99.7
Finding percentiles and probabilities using z-scores
lecture_notes.demonstration: Converting raw SAT scores to z-scores and finding the percentage of students scoring above a threshold

review.value: z = 1.5 means the value is 1.5 standard deviations above the mean. Negative z means below the mean. z = 0 is the mean.
The table gives the area to the left of a z-score. For P(Z > z), subtract from 1. For P(a < Z < b), find both areas and subtract.

class_schedule.topic: Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and One-Sample Tests
learning_objectives: Conduct a one-sample z-test for a population mean

lecture_notes.introduction: Hypothesis testing is the formal framework for using sample data to make decisions about population claims. It is the engine of scientific research and evidence-based policy.
key_concepts: Null hypothesis (H₀): the claim being tested, assumed true until evidence says otherwise
Alternative hypothesis (H₁): what we conclude if we reject H₀
Significance level (α): the threshold for rejecting H₀, typically 0.05
P-value: probability of observing data this extreme if H₀ is true
Type I error (false positive) and Type II error (false negative)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Testing the claim that a new tutoring program increases average exam scores above the historical mean of 72

review.value: If p-value ≤ α, reject H₀. If p-value > α, fail to reject H₀. We never 'accept' H₀; we only fail to find sufficient evidence against it.

review.key: Type I vs. Type II Error
review.value: Type I: rejecting H₀ when it's true (false alarm). Type II: failing to reject H₀ when it's false (missed finding). Reducing one increases the other.

questions.question: A dataset of 9 home prices (in thousands): 150, 175, 180, 185, 190, 195, 200, 210, 850. Calculate the mean and median. Which measure better represents the typical home price and why?
questions.answer: Mean = (150+175+180+185+190+195+200+210+850)/9 = 2335/9 = $259,444. Median = 5th value when sorted = $190,000. The median ($190,000) better represents the typical home price because the mean ($259,444) is pulled upward by the outlier ($850,000). In right-skewed distributions like housing prices, the median is resistant to extreme values and reflects the center of the majority of the data.

rubric.key: Correct choice with explanation of outlier effect

questions.question: Explain the five-number summary and describe how you would use it to identify outliers in a dataset. What specific threshold determines whether a data point is an outlier?
questions.answer: The five-number summary consists of the minimum, first quartile (Q1), median (Q2), third quartile (Q3), and maximum. The interquartile range is IQR = Q3 - Q1. A data point is an outlier if it is below Q1 - 1.5 × IQR or above Q3 + 1.5 × IQR. These boundaries are called fences. Outliers appear as individual dots beyond the whiskers in a box plot.

rubric.key: Correct 1.5 × IQR outlier threshold with both boundaries

questions.question: The scores on a standardized test are normally distributed with a mean of 500 and a standard deviation of 100. What percentage of students score between 400 and 700? Show your z-score calculations.

rubric.key: Correct final probability with subtraction shown

questions.question: A company claims their batteries last an average of 500 hours. You test 36 batteries and find a sample mean of 490 hours with a known population standard deviation of 30 hours. At the 0.05 significance level, is there evidence the true mean is less than 500 hours? State the hypotheses, calculate the test statistic, and state your conclusion.
questions.answer: H₀: μ = 500 (batteries last 500 hours on average). H₁: μ < 500 (batteries last less than claimed). This is a left-tailed test. Test statistic: z = (490 - 500)/(30/√36) = -10/5 = -2.00. Critical value at α = 0.05 (left-tailed): z = -1.645. Since -2.00 < -1.645, we reject H₀. P-value = P(Z < -2.00) = 0.0228 < 0.05. There is sufficient evidence at the 0.05 level to conclude that the true average battery life is less than 500 hours.

rubric.key: Correct conclusion with proper statistical language

major_assignments.title: Real-World Data Analysis Project
major_assignments.description: Select a real-world dataset from an approved source (government data portal, Kaggle, or research repository) and conduct a comprehensive statistical analysis. Your analysis must include descriptive statistics, data visualization, probability calculations, and a hypothesis test with interpretation.
objectives: Apply descriptive statistics to summarize a real-world dataset
Create meaningful data visualizations that reveal patterns
Formulate and test a hypothesis using sample data
Communicate statistical findings clearly to a non-technical audience
deliverables: Descriptive statistics summary: measures of center and spread for key variables
At least 4 visualizations: histogram, box plot, scatter plot, and one additional
Hypothesis test with full write-up: hypotheses, test statistic, p-value, conclusion
Written report (1500-2000 words) interpreting findings for a general audience
Spreadsheet with all calculations and formulas visible

criteria: Appropriate measures calculated for each variable type
Correct calculations verified in spreadsheet
Meaningful interpretation of what statistics reveal
Discussion of outliers and distribution shape
At least 4 correctly constructed graphs
Appropriate graph type chosen for each variable
Clear labels, titles, and formatting
Visualizations reveal genuine patterns in the data
Appropriate hypotheses formulated
Correct test statistic and p-value calculated
Conclusion stated in context of the research question
Limitations and potential errors acknowledged
Findings explained in accessible language
Statistical terms defined when used
Conclusions supported by the analysis
Professional formatting and error-free writing
Comprehensive analysis of a real-world dataset using all course techniques
Correct statistical calculations with clear documentation
Meaningful visualizations that support the narrative
Well-written report that communicates findings to a general audience

homework.description: Weekly problem sets practicing statistical calculations and interpretation

quizzes.description: Regular assessment of statistical concepts and computational skills

data_analysis_project.description: Comprehensive analysis of a real-world dataset using all course techniques

midterm_exam.description: Covers descriptive statistics, probability, and normal distribution

final_exam.description: Cumulative exam with emphasis on inferential statistics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is important for lab work and data analysis activities. More than 3 unexcused absences will reduce participation grade by 5% per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Homework is due at the start of class. One day late receives 50% credit; no credit after one day. The project has staged deadlines that must be met.
course_policies.collaboration: You may discuss homework approaches with classmates but must submit your own calculations. The data analysis project is individual work with a unique dataset.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Fabricating data or copying statistical analyses is academic dishonesty. All work must use your assigned dataset and your own calculations.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Spreadsheet software (Excel or Google Sheets) required. Access to a statistical calculator. Optional but recommended: R or Python for advanced students.
